> I think this situation may not just be applicable to the BR devices (and/or NCP model or posix/linux TUN netif). I think it can happen on all device types (end-devices) and in other situations (e.g., with lwip netif, or other use-cases).

Essentially, I think it's correct to forward the message equally no matter it's initiated in Thread stack or from Host. (@bukepo may have comments on it) So, this PR is a step in that direction. 

> Does the #7175 require that "message given from `otIp6Send()` to be passed in `otIp6ReceiveCallback` callback"?

Yes. In #7175, SBBR has subscribed an multicast address MA1, and the Host on Backbone Link is pining MA1. 
When SBBR receives the ping request to MA1 in **kernel**, kernel sends ping reply via wpan0 to Thread stack. Now, Thread stack will choose SBBR itself as the Border Router because the destination of ping reply is an on-link address, which SBBR has added as an external route. Currently, SBBR failed to foward the ping reply because:
1) SBBR can't forward the ping reply back to TUN due to `aFromNcpHost` checks
2) SBBR can't forward the ping reply to another BR because it naturally choose itself as the BR

So, SBBR need to either forward the ping reply back to TUN, or to another BR. 
This PR tries to fix 1) and I think the change is obvious and clean. 
I think @edmont tried to fix 2) in #7193 but it seemed to be quite complex. 

Note that 1) is the best route, while 2) is routing the message with much higher cost. So, 1) should be the correct behavior. 
So, I think this PR is indeed a bugfix.

> mmm what would that look like? I imagine that many formats still have their own specific keyword argument

@almarklein Sure, you can just append those to the call and they will get sorted out by the plugin or the backend, e.g., `iio.imread("some.jpg", plugin="pillow", rotate=False)`. If there are a lot of backend-specific ones like there are for ffmpeg a plugin can specify `**kwargs` in its implementation of the interface and then forward them appropriately or throw an Exception for unsupported ones.

If you were/are wondering how it will look like on the documentation side then that will depend on the individual plugin/backend. Each plugin has its module parsed by autosummary/autodoc and then it can decide how it wants to be structured. At the moment all plugins are single page and follow numpydoc style where possible, but it doesn't have to be like this. You could have a `Format Specific Parameters` section where you list the parameters that are unique to a format if there are many, or you can simply list them in a single `Parameters` list and flag them appropriately (what I've been doing so far since it's only a few parameters if any). 

The main point is that if you use a plugin, say `plugin=dicom`, then the DICOM plugin API reference is _the_ place to search for any parameters; format-specific or otherwise. No more need to start digging through Supported Formats (this info doesn't really belong there anyway). Supported Formats will instead list what format is read by which plugin; if you wonder "(Can imageio read)/(Does imageio support) X " (for X = RTSP streams :D https://github.com/imageio/imageio/issues/658) then you go to Supported Formats, Crtl + F for the extension or format name, and check which plugin you need to install.

If you _need_ to read a specific file with a specific plugin using as a specific format and the format is - for whatever reason - different from the format that the backend detects (via extension magic number or otherwise), then you need to check the plugin docs regarding the keyword that forces this particular backend to interpret the specific file as a specific format. This does seem rather niche, so I think it is okay that the call is a bit more sophisticated (`iio.imread(specific_file, plugin="specific_plugin", kwarg_enforcing_format=...)`.

---

> this list seems like it's easy to generate from the information that is already available

Yes, partially. In the legacy plugin manager a plugin must explicitly register formats, which I guess we could tap into to populate the list. The new plugin manager currently doesn't care about formats (by design) and sees them as more of an optional optimization (https://github.com/imageio/imageio/issues/641) than mandatory information. We will need to write some kind of dict/json file/thing for https://github.com/imageio/imageio/issues/641 that signals which plugins to prioritize based on file extension. We could again use this to populate the list.

What we can't easily automate is the association of an extension with its full format name (and link to additional documentation).  I think this information is important because it clarifies what an extension is and it (may) de-alias formats that share the same extensions. The only way I can think of maintaining this is to template the list and then simply populate pre-existing entries based on the extension-plugin pairs we get from somewhere. We will still have to manually add new formats and we won't gain guarantees on completeness (since backends might introduce new formats without us being aware for a while). The library will support those new formats immediately without changes, but the docs will lag behind. Considering this, automation didn't offer enough bang for the buck imo, so I just wrote a manual one.

That said, I'm happy to automate if you have a better idea.

There were three reasons behind the ready-to-use example, including both backends, that I suggested:

1. Most projects don't override `AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS`. It isn't in the default generated settings file (unlike `MIDDLEWARE`). By giving the full content, I save users a lookup of the default value in Django's docs.
2. This discussion started from — it is too easy to replace `"django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend"` with `"sesame.backends.ModelBackend"` instead of adding `"sesame.backends.ModelBackend"`. We wanted to clarify that the most common usage is to include both.
3. While it is technically possible to remove `"django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend"` from `AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS` and`"django.contrib.auth.middleware.AuthenticationMiddleware"` from `MIDDLEWARE`, this isn't very common. Often you'll keep them at least for the admin.

----

If we wanted to provide additional guidance in prose, it would be:

> Since `"django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend"` and `"sesame.backends.ModelBackend"` take different parameters, no set of credentials is valid for both, so the order doesn't matter.

I feel like this would create more confusion than clarity and those who want the answer can find it easily in Django's docs, which state:

> The order of [AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/4.0/ref/settings/#std:setting-AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS) matters, so if the same username and password is valid in multiple backends, Django will stop processing at the first positive match.

I thought about it a couple of time, and potentially we could also do things a little better. Here is the state of my analysis so far.

So, `TypeVar` is used for two usecases:
* being the type variable of a generic class (eg. `class MyList(Generic[T])` with `T`  a `TypeVar` instance)
* being the type variable for a generic method (typically `def add(one: A, two: A) -> A`)

We are talking here about the second case, which is to potentially take advantage of genericity for the `TypedJSONObjectWithFields.from_json` method and by extension the overriden method in `Challenge`.

In `JSONObjectWithFields.from_json` the genericity is possible, because the method "returns an instance of the class on which `from_json` is called", which is expressed by a `TypeVar` instance type on the `cls` parameter and its returned type (`def from_json(cls: Type[T], ...) -> T`), and this instructs mypy to dynamically infer the actual type of the method in sub-classes calls (eg. `from_json` effectively returns `SubJson` if `SubJson` is a child class of `JSONObjectWithFields`) when types are checked.

However `TypedJSONObjectWithFields.from_json` implementation and purpose slightly differs from `JSONObjectWithFields.from_json`. It is "returns an instance of a registered subclass of the current class given the `dict` provided by the method" (with internally a resolution done from the field `type` in the `dict`).

So potentially, we could declare a `TypeVar` bound to `TypedJSONObjectWithFields` in josepy, and use it to make `register`, `get_type_cls` and `from_json` generic, which means here accepting/returning any sub-type of the current class type.

I think it is representing the actual usecase of `TypedJSONObjectWithFields`, what do you think ?

In the AWS job store, when we batch up the updates, we collect all the updates that need to be made and then we make [one call into the AWS Python module](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/de3ecf6d22dea55752d1487ac9957527fc03aa27/src/toil/jobStores/aws/jobStore.py#L315) with the whole batch. This goes back to the recent slack thread about why Bazel has both `deps` and `data`: a consumer classifies how the dep should be treated... in this case, the `pytest` rules could have a special argument for these dependencies to indicate that they should be treated as installed distributions rather than sources.

> not following explicit deps through a python_distribution target when computing the transitive closure.

Pants has started to go down this route via `special_cased_dependencies`, although the support is nascent (...as the name suggests), and is not type-aware. See https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/12251#discussion_r667079976 for some discussion and see [the implementation](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/7a5205aa23874bd67f7b00acd6b060684d778e0d/src/python/pants/engine/internals/graph.py#L866-L871).

That parameter essentially amounts to a boolean filter of dependencies that is applied while computing either direct or transitive dependencies. I say that it's "not type aware" because only a consumer who is aware that a particular target type has `SpecialCasedDependencies`-subclassed fields will actually be able to consume them "correctly", whereas I could imagine an API where `DependenciesRequest` or `TransitiveTargetRequest` optionally take a list of fields to expand dependencies from (with a default of just `Dependencies`), and the more widely used they are, the more likely that is to be necessary.

@Eric-Arellano would know more. But it seems like two options would be:
1. `python_distribution` marking its own `dependencies` field `SpecialCasedDependencies` and adjusting how it requests them
    * this would impact all consumers of `python_distribution` targets: they would not (by default) get the full transitive deps of that target: they'd instead get "whatever behavior we want when a `python_library` depends on a `python_distribution`" 
2. `python_tests`/`python_library`/etc gaining a special-cased field to which you could add things that you wanted to treat as installed distributions rather than sources.

I think that given that we have the `python_distribution` target which already "marks" a particular intended behavior, option 2 would be redundant. Option 1 would also be more consistent (haven't looked at the code, but I think it's the path that you're already on here): support for Python code consuming `python_distribution`s would need to be added ~everywhere.

IMO it's better not to expose details like this that are only needed for recursive calls in the function signature that's exposed to callers, since they shouldn't ever set `num_remaining_tries` or `num_tries_so_far`.

Instead we can just have two dedicated smaller functions nested inside this one, which close over the unchanging parts of the recursion (like the request and the API method and the clock) without having to pass them as arguments in the recursion; rather, we only pass arguments for the state that needs to be updated.  E.g. like this:

```python
def async_call_with_retries(
    api_method,
    request,
    done_callback,
    clock=None,
):
    if clock is None:
        clock = time
    logger.debug("Async RPC call %s with request: %r", rpc_name, request)

    def async_call(handler):
        api_method.future(
            request,
            timeout=_GRPC_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECS,
            metadata=version_metadata(),
        ).add_done_callback(handler)

    def retry_handler(future, num_attempts):
        e = future.exception()
        if e is None:
            done_callback(future)
            return
        else:
            logger.info("RPC call %s got error %s", rpc_name, e)
            # If unable to retry, proceed to completion_handler.
            if e.code() not in _GRPC_RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES:
                done_callback(future)
                return
            if num_attempts >= _GRPC_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS:
                done_callback(future)
                return
            # If able to retry, wait then do so.
            backoff_secs = _compute_backoff_seconds(num_attempts + 1)
            clock.sleep(backoff_secs)
            async_call(functools.partial(retry_handler, num_attempts=num_attempts + 1))
            
    return async_call(functools.partial(retry_handler, num_attempts=1))

@nickjong Looks like all toolkits except for the Object Detector and Style Transfer take `max_iterations` to mean "Maximum number of Epochs". However, OD and Style Transfer take it to mean "Maximum number of training iterations ~ number of mini-batches". While the latter is more correct from an academic perspective, I have modified the drawing classifier to take `max_iterations` to mean "Maximum number of Epochs" for two reasons, after discussing with @ylow and @TobyRoseman :
1. An epoch is a more intuitive parameter to pass in for our users.
2. A default value for number of epochs makes more sense for this toolkit, just like it does for the Image Classifier.
3. To align with other toolkits, especially the Image Classifier.

It is a unfortunate that the same parameter name means different things for different toolkits. There are three possible solutions, all API-breaking changes outside the scope of this PR that we should seriously think about before executing:
1. Rename the parameter from `max_iterations` to `max_epochs` for all toolkits except ST/OD. 
2. Maybe change OD and ST logic to also take in number of epochs but that may be suboptimal for the respective toolkits. 
3. For OD and ST, rename the parameter from `max_iterations` to `max_mini_batches` or something distinct from `max_iterations` to not cause confusion.

Note that for any parameter renames, there is a backwards-compatibility supporting path through `kwargs`, as pointed out by @ylow .

Ah, the root of this may be the difference in how the CUDA implementation (in `devIoU()`) and the CPU implementation (in `nms_cpu_kernel()`) determine whether the IoU for a box-pair is over threshold.

The `devIoU()` code multiplies total area by the threshold then compares to the intersection:

```
  return interS > threshold * (Sa + Sb - interS);
``

While `nms_cpu_kernel()` divides intersection by total and then compares to the threshold:

```
      auto ovr = inter / (iarea + areas[j] - inter);
      if (ovr > iou_threshold)
```

Those calculations give opposite results for some of the inputs that were being generated by the test. Here's a little C program that uses the guts of `devIoU()` to demonstrate:

```
$ cat iou.c   
#include <stdio.h>                           

#define MIN(x, y) (x) < (y) ? (x) : (y)      
#define MAX(x, y) (x) > (y) ? (x) : (y)      

int main(void) {                             
    float a[4] = {0.426352024078, 10.556936264038, 29.010599136353, 13.252419471741};     
    float b[4] = {0.426352024078, 10.556936264038, 36.156661987305, 13.252419471741};     
    float threshold = 0.8;                   

    float left = MAX(a[0], b[0]), right = MIN(a[2], b[2]);                                
    float top = MAX(a[1], b[1]), bottom = MIN(a[3], b[3]);                                
    float width = MAX(right - left, (float)0), height = MAX(bottom - top, (float)0);      
    float interS = width * height;           
    float Sa = (a[2] - a[0]) * (a[3] - a[1]);                                             
    float Sb = (b[2] - b[0]) * (b[3] - b[1]);                                             

    int devIoU_ret = interS > threshold * (Sa + Sb - interS); /* devIoU */                

    float ovr = interS / (Sa + Sb - interS); 

    printf("devIoU:         %d\n", devIoU_ret);                                           
    printf("nms_cpu_kernel: %d\n", ovr > threshold);                                      
}
```

And:

```
$ ./a.out
devIoU:         0
nms_cpu_kernel: 1
```

Note this is running entirely on the CPU. The fact that CUDA's involved in the test here appears to be a red herring.

I _think_ the `nms_cpu_kernel()` implementation is the better one, with the rationale being that the operands to the division are more likely to be around the same magnitude (vs the threshold and area coming from 2 different domains in `devIoU()`'s multiplication).

And the `nms_cpu_kernel()` implementation also better matches the python code in `box_iou()` so would presumably be more consistent with that as well.

On the other hand, adjusting `devIoU()` to match `nms_cpu_kernel()` would introduce a division into the CUDA code. I'm not sure whether that's a problem.

I understand your reasoning but I would like to make it work like before for pragmatic reasons. I would like to make it easier for the user. It is idiomatic in Python to not handle cases where `None` should be returned and as you correctly observed it is a Python syntactic sugar for `return None` but Python programmers are used to this way of work. So, you just have to handle cases you care about in the function and leave other branches "empty". Insisting on `return []` on unsuccessful matches is not idiomatic Python and puts additional burden on lib users. And in tight loops it creates more overhead as creating a list is more expensive than returning None. I would say that your background is in functional languages where this line of reasoning is the norm (and BTW I do agree from the theoretical point of view :) ).

Recognizers are not much different as they receive a sequence and should return a matched subsequence from the current position. That might be a string but also can be a sequence of any other objects. So, we could reason here the same way, since we should return a matched subsequence it should be an empty sequence/list if nothing matches but we return `None` for pragmatic reasons.

OpenThread currently expects that the alarm will fire if `t0 + dt` is <= now. This also applies when `dt` is `0`.

It is true that OpenThread may call `otPlatAlarmMilliStartAt()` with `dt = 0`. While we could update OpenThread to only make calls with `dt > 0`, there is still no guarantee that `otPlatAlarmMilliStartAt()` will be called with a `t0 + dt` that happens in the future. For example, the platform may delay OpenThread's execution just before the call to `otPlatAlarmMilliStartAt()`.

> Building from source does use pip, but does not use PyPi.

It's true that using `pip` generally doesn't rely on a package repository like PyPI and that it can be used with local files. However, all of the commands in the section you've linked are specifically about how to build from source with a package served by PyPI.

Notice that none of the commands in that section include a filepath, like `.` or `python-package/`. And also notice that if you try one of those commands, you'll see a warning like the following:

> UserWarning: Disabling all use of wheels due to the use of --build-option / --global-option / --install-option

So a command like `pip install lightgbm --install-option=--cuda` means "download the source (non-wheel) distribution from PyPI, and build a version of the Python package using a `lib_lightgbm.so` compiled with `-DUSE_CUDA=ON`".

If you scroll down a bit further, you'll see that there is a separate section, https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/tree/master/python-package#install-from-github, that covers building from source in a local clone of this git repo.

> We can easily compensate for the code size increase for one more parameter if the action strings are reused? Especially when the action strings are not only shared in meshcop, but also reused for other modules (e.g. MLE).

In general yes, but not necessarily in all cases. There are counter-intuitive cases.
It depends on different factors, e.g., size of strings being shared, how the new arg is added. 

Let me give an example:

If we share `"send"` string,  we would save 4 chars or 4 bytes (per its use). But adding a new parameter/argument in the `LogError()` call can add more than 4 bytes (6 bytes or more on arm-gcc) to the generated code (again per use).

   1197c:       00045930        .word   0x00045930
```

Note that we see two pointers being loaded into `r0` and `r1` before calling `LogError()`. We need two bytes for `ldr  r1, [pc, #40]` instruction for the second argument, but in addition to this, the actual pointer value is stored right at the end of function at `[pc, #40]` or at address `11978` (which stores the string address `00456aa`). This adds another 4 bytes to the code. 

---

Another good (counter-intuitive) example is our logging:
- We have the feature to append log level and/or log region prefix string to the log.
- The prefix string are literally added to the `format` string, so the strings (in the image) are like:
  - `"[NOTE]-MLE-----: RLOC16 %04x -> %04x"`, or `"[NOTE]-MLE-----: Downgrade to REED"`
- At first it seems to be inefficient and makes sense to change it to reuse the `"[NOTE]"` and `"-MLE----"` strings.
- We tried this, which boils down to a call like this:
   - `otPlatLog("%s%s: " aFormat, sLevelPrefix[aLevel], sRegionPrefix[aRegion], ...)`
   - `sLevelPrefix[]` is const array of strings (for each log level), same way for `sRegionPrefix[]` string array.
- This change actually increased the code size largely (by ~2K if I recall).
- The reason is the overhead of adding two arguments to `otPlatLog()` call (which is variable arg function itself) was more than the bytes/chars saved by sharing the string literals.

-----

> But since -Os is used, compilers should prefer smaller code than than higher performance? I think we can have the benefit of reduced code size on majority of compilers (at least GCC) even without using string enum. The benefit should already overrun the potential risk.

Over the years, I have been surprised many times by toolchain behaviors (specially in embedded sw where we have many custom/specialized compilers). My approach/advice is that it is always better not to rely on toolchains and/or assume any behavior. Better to be explicit in the code (when/if we can).

The check does make sense. When given a list of ICs, Pants (and Pex) inserts a logical `OR` between them in some operations. Perhaps only when searching for compatible interpreters on the local system IIRC. That "feature" should be dropped, but is still laying around. It has zero support in the PyPA ecosystem though and cannot be represented there where there is only the `,` operator in an individual IC for logical `AND`. So anyone with multiple ICs that wants to publish a dist needs to convert those into a single IC by using exclusions. For example: [">=2.7,<3", ">=3.5,<3.11"] (Pex's own constraints), should be converted by the user to ["">=2.7,!=3.0.*,!=3.1.*,!=3.2.*,!=3.3.*,!=3.4.*,<3.11"]. It turns out that the explicit `not np.isnan(cval)` is redundant because the `min_func(output_image) <= cval <= max_func(output_image)` can only be true if cval is not NaN.

Surprisingly, it turns out that even if you remove both checks, the rest of the new code as written happens to still pass the test for when `cval` is NaN due to a later quirk.  `cval` will be NaN for the `clip()` call, but that doesn't break the clipping because `min(min_val, np.nan)` evaluates to `min_val`.  I say "surprisingly" because not only does `np.min([min_val, np.nan])` evaluate to `np.nan`, `min(np.nan, min_val)` (i.e., swapping the order of the arguments) evaluates to `np.nan` as well.  I wasn't aware of that consequence of comparison operators for `np.nan` always evaluating to `False`: `min(min_val, np.nan) != min(np.nan, min_val)`.

So, while it is fine to have `_process: Process = None` as a class attribute **and still maintain different `_process` instances on the instance level** it would fall apart to have something like `_process_list: List[Process] = []` on the class level if the reference isn't *overwritten* but *mutated* in instances e.g. by  `self._process_list.append(some_process)`. In that case, that process would appear in the `_process_list` of all other plugin instances as well.

With that in mind, class level attributes have more legitimate usage that I thought they have.

I have a question about the ABI compatibility of std::functions.

std::functions is a STL container, which has different implementation between different versions of compiler (e.g. gcc4 and gcc5) or different compilers (gcc and clang).

For example, MXNet is built in gcc4, which uses the reference & to store the function arguments. However, the other application is built in gcc5, which uses the rvalue reference && to store the function arguments. The other application could not call the MXNet API by PackedFunc, since the implementations of std::function are different between gcc4 and gcc5.

These tend to reason about dispatch to other task queues as "scheduling", but for us, it will probably suffice just to worry about hopping threads (not time delays and such).

So in the example that you specify, you would typically set each subscriber to receive elements on a different queue. Something like:
```
iterator->ReceiveOn(thread1)->Subscribe(subscriber1);
iterator->ReceiveOn(thread2)->Subscribe(subscriber2);
```
Effectively the middle "pipe" in this pipeline just switches to the desired thread before sending the value along

the primary style i take is one where things feel comfortable.  When the previous line has to wrap, as above, it already feels cramped and cluttered to me as somethin large to wrap my mind around. --

Regardless, i find that these style questions *really* slow down PRs.  I'm already 100% on board with the linter stopping things and having to fit that style.  Having to rigidly conform on a bunch of *unstated* things is more difficult to deal with and ends up taking a lot of time to deal with.  i.e. if i chance this i have ot wait another hour, babysit hte PR and whatnot.  I'd really prefer we be able to find a reasonable balance here.  I'm trying to keep the code readable and clear, without gross deviation.  But i'm likely never going to exactly match how everyone else has their personal preferences on things.

Note, my ideal here would be: if this is something we want to codify, we just do so with a tool.  Then there's no guesswork.  I can simply run the tool and get the output that is desired.  That can be done preemptively, not in the middle of the PR.

I just finished playing with this a bit.

When following the instructions here (or the most intuitive flow on Azure DevOps), Azure tries to set up an Oauth app in addition to the GitHub app we initially installed. This Oauth app is where the concerning permissions about write access to all aspects of your repository come in.

I tried to look into what these repository permissions allows Azure to do but couldn't find much. I think they correspond to the repository scope described [here](https://developer.github.com/apps/building-oauth-apps/understanding-scopes-for-oauth-apps/) but I couldn't find anything about what exactly that entails.

(As a slightly unrelated aside, I looked into the permissions that Travis' Oauth app requires and it is:
```
Organizations and teams
Read-only access
This application will be able to read your organization, team membership, and private project boards.

Commit statuses
Read and write access
This application will be able to read and write commit statuses (no direct code access).

Deployments
Manage deployments and deployment status
This application will be able to manage deployments and deployment status (no direct code access).

Personal user data
Email addresses (read-only)
This application will be able to read your private email addresses.

Repository webhooks and services
Read and write access
This application will be able to read and modify repository webhooks and services (no direct code access).
```
which is much more limited.)

I'm still personally a bit hesitant to grant the permissions on this Oauth app at least without a better understanding of what it can do, but there may be good news. The need to install this second application seems to me like a bug. [This page](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/pipelines/repos/github?view=azure-devops&tabs=yaml#grant-access-to-your-github-repositories) talks about how the GitHub application should be preferred over the Oauth app for setting up CI. This Oauth app also isn't referenced at all at https://www.azuredevopslabs.com/labs/azuredevops/github-integration/. When you initially create the pipeline following the instructions there, you don't need the Oauth app.

I'm not the only one that thinks this is a bug either, however, it appears Microsoft disagrees as they closed https://developercommunity.visualstudio.com/content/problem/642369/pipelines-creation-falls-back-to-github-oauth-auth.html as "Not a bug". Despite that, they describe a way to work around the problem. You can click the button circled here:

![Screen Shot 2019-09-24 at 4 56 19 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6504915/65558627-67679300-deec-11e9-9bb2-457b0512c9b2.png)

This button says it is to create a pipeline without a YAML, but it lets you use your GitHub app connection to link to GitHub and then the first option is to use a YAML. I tested this a bit on my fork and this seems to work.

Do you think these permissions are sufficient for us now and in the future or do you think we will also need/want to set up the Oauth app? I'll flag for you that https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/pipelines/repos/github?view=azure-devops&tabs=yaml#grant-access-to-your-github-repositories which I linked above says:

> The Azure Pipelines GitHub App is the recommended authentication type for continuous integration pipelines. For release pipelines that are triggered by changes to a GitHub repository, use OAuth or personal access token authentication.

I was surprised too, but it makes sense - if you a bootstrapping a certificate chain, you don't want to get into a situation where you need a different certificate to make the SSL connection to get a certificate...

[RFC 5280](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280#section-4.2.2.1) doesn't say HTTPS is forbidden, but does say 

> Conforming applications that support HTTP or FTP for accessing certificates MUST be able to accept individual DER encoded certificates and SHOULD be able to accept "certs-only" CMS messages.

and
 
> When the id-ad-caIssuers accessMethod is used, at least one instance SHOULD specify an accessLocation that is an HTTP [RFC2616] or LDAP [RFC4516] URI.

There are plenty of cases in the spec (FTP, LDAP, certs-only message instead of DER) that would be conforming but that we wouldn't parse. The fall-though logic will at least log the Authority Access Info so we can see what to add (or just download manually). Who knows, `https:` may show up some day...

I think the main difference is that the TrackedCheckpoint is also used to track checkpoints on remote nodes that are potentially never serialized and sent to the driver. The `ray.ml.Checkpoint` doesn't know about checkpoints on remote nodes. We've discussed introducing a custom URI like `node://<node_ip>/path/on/remote/node` but came to the conclusion that this is not well defined and opens a lot of surface area for potential bugs relating to checkpoint persistence if this is a user-facing abstraction.

Another reason is that tracked checkpoints are included in the experiment checkpoints (i.e. the TrialRunner state) and thus would be doubly serialized all the time. The alternatives here are not to use the current checkpoint directory structure at all but always and only checkpoint to the trial runner state, which is not great for various reasons (can elaborate on this more if needed). The other alternative would be to special case this, but again, then there is no point of using the Checkpoint class to begin with.

Maybe as a counter question, what would we gain from using `ray.ml.Checkpoint` here instead of a a dict or string?

So making a copy of data always has a performance penalty versus not, as the computer has to allocate new memory (which sometimes has to call out to the kernel and punt your application to the back of the kernel scheduler's line) and then copy element-by-element over. That's why I suggested having an option to turn immutability off.

I understand the value of immutability for reasoning about code, but when the language doesn't understand that concept at a fundamental level where it can bake in optimizations (like under-the-hood mutating a data structure because it knows the original is never referenced again), it causes a lot of memory thrashing.

I normally wouldn't bring this up for the Python bindings since I don't believe Python is the right language to use if you care for performance, but since this Cython rewrite with baked-in support for Numpy and raw memory blocks is very much about improving the performance of the Python bindings, I think it's worth keeping the last potential bottleneck on performance as an optional behavior, especially since immutability wasn't enforced before.

I don't have a strong immediate use case, but it seemed to me that passing the `super` object was both not very useful and inconsistent with other uses of both `super`. The practical uses I could think of for today all work best with `__self__` unwrapped.

It's inconsistent because when `super(Class, self).method()` is executing, the `self` that's sent to `method` isn't the `super` object, it's straight up exactly the `self` object provided to `super` (because `super` isn't particularly useful; see next paragraph).  Adapting on `super` ought to implicitly behave the same simply to match that expectation.

It's not particularly useful because the `super` object cannot access instance variables of `self` (only class descriptors/methods), nor (as a consequence) can it discover any interfaces that are `directlyProvidedBy` the `self` object. It has no MRO or type and `super` doesn't work on it, so you'd only be able to try this trick for exactly one level of hierarchy. The receiving factory would need to unwrap `__self__` in short order to do any of those things…and at that point, you're writing an adapter that's customized to know it's working with `super`, which destroys reusability: given `class Base` and `class Derived(Base)` the same adapter can't work for `super(Derived, self)` *and* `Base()`.

If one wanted to use the `super` object in an adapter, I think the better thing to do would be to register a multi-adapter on `(Interface-or-class, type)` (called with `component.getMultiAdapter(IAdapter, (self, Derived))`) or possibly `(Interface-or-class, super)`. The latter would work today (but not after this change), while the former would work today and after this change.

> I'm not a fan of forcing the keyword. Its makes the interface not like dict 

That's the precise reason I'm enforcing a kwarg: this interface is very similar to `dict` but is subtly different. Subtle differences like this are the origin of many bugs.

I believe it's going to be a common gotcha that people think the second parameter determines the default return value when in reality it sets the default value passed to the constructor for that Field. The kwarg reduces that risk for confusion.

> The Any is a shame, but I know of no way to deal with this without mypy supporting recursive self types. 

Agreed, but I can't think of any way around it as you describe. The important thing to me is that we return `_F`, which is what allows MyPy to know that `tgt.get(Compatibility).value` is `Tuple[str, ...]` whereas `tgt.get(ZipSafe).value` is `bool` because `Compatibility` and `ZipSafe` are fully typed classes.

This imprecise type hint is another reason I think the kwarg is worth the verbosity. We don't have MyPy/type hints to guide the user that this is wrong: 

```python
default_compatibility = Compatibility('CPython==3.6.*', address=tgt.address)
print(tgt.get(Compatibility, default_compatibility))
```

They would only know it's wrong at runtime because we would end up calling the constructor `Compatibility(raw_value=default_compatibility)`, which is wrong.

Right, this is one of my grievances with the PED-RPC protocol. It strictly assumes that one message is sent per connection, which doesn't work too well with restoring options when a new server connection is detected. _hot_transmit is a hack to call options on the remote RPC service which uses the current connection opened by the command that detected a new server, sets an option and resets the connection. This is done for every option that is set, so it is called a total of n times if n options are set, leaving the logic in a state where a connection is open (thus the "hot" part of _hot_transmit) to be then resumed normally with the call of the RPC function that was originally called.

Also, this logic shamelessly assumes that the RPC server is not restarted while restoring options. I think this is reasonable, because it would complicate things even more if we were to check this here as well. This is one of the main reasons why I -- if I had a business case -- would scrap the implementation and do something with RPyC instead as it is *much* easier to work with in the end.

Right now, this is just a helper to avoid the boiler-plate unwrapping and re-wrapping. However, we could expand this in two ways to be able to use the default behavior in 99% of the cases, without implementing an intermediate layer:

1. We could a `select_parameters` parameter to `register_kernel` that covers the use case if the kernel we want to register only uses a subset of parameters of the dispatcher.
2. We could add automatic handling for datapoints that carry metadata (currently only `BoundingBox`):
   1. We can inspect the `kernel` to find metadata that needs to be passed explicitly. This can happen when registering and thus only once and without runtime performance regression. At runtime we use this information and adjust the `kwargs` before calling the kernel.
   2. We can handle returned metadata. For `BoundingBox` we know that the kernel output is either `Tensor` or `Tuple[Tensor, Tuple[int, int]]`. In the second case, the second element in the returned tuple is the `spatial_size`, which we could in the returned `BoundingBox`.

Going for that would make our implementation quite a bit simpler. However this also has two downsides:

1. Users that look at our code on how to write custom kernels for their datapoints, will likely miss that their is an intermediate layer in the first place.
2. The automatic returned metadata handling (2.ii above) will only work for builtin datapoints.

## Wrapped validator
I rewrote some stuff to match the expected signature:
```py
from typing import Annotated, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError, WrapValidator, field_validator


def validate_char(cls, v) -> Optional[str]:
    # truncate strings to their first character
    if isinstance(v, str) and len(v) > 1:
        return v[0]

    return v


def wrapped_validate_char(v, handler) -> Optional[str]:
    try:
        return validate_char(None, v)  # Try custom validator
    except ValidationError:
        return handler(v)  # Pydantic validator
    finally:
        return "X"  # Manually set default value

class Model(BaseModel):
    char_1: str = Field(description="A single character", min_length=1, max_length=1)
    char_2: str = Field(description="A single character", min_length=1, max_length=1)

    # Wrap can only be set on the annotation, not on the field validator function
    char_3: Annotated[str, WrapValidator(wrapped_validate_char)] = Field(description="A single character", min_length=1, max_length=1)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    my_model = Model(
        char_1="A",
        char_2="B",
        char_3="CCC"
    )

    print(my_model)
```

Is there a reason why you are setting default values to `None` rather than `True`/`False`?

Using a default value of `None` is recommended if the default would otherwise be a mutable variables (e.g., an empty `list` or `dict`), but booleans are immutable. It is clearer to use `True`/`False` directly than to set them inside the method.

Sometimes there are legitimate use-cases for defaulting a boolean variable to `None`, for detecting if a parameter was explicitly passed or not. But I don't think they apply here. (And if they do apply in the future, you can always change the default value later.)

Honestly, I feel like this is a very fragile way to determine created resources; Any changes in the printed messages will break these tests. It is a high maintenance effort. Can we instead use CloudFormation's describe-stack-resources operation ?

Insisting that _all_ default values are `None` really is not standard practice in Python (e.g., you won't find it in Google's Python Style Guide). It doesn't really matter, but you might try inquiring with g/python-users or g/python-style for opinions of other experienced Python programmers.

Here's my take:

1. This extensibility concern about keeping default values in sync is only relevant if you're using _implementation inheritance_, but implementation inheritance is a maintenance nightmare that should be avoided in almost all case. If you're not using implementation inheritance, then you'll have to duplicate the logic for default values anyways.
2. Open source and data science users in particular are much more likely to see public _interfaces_ for code (e.g., with auto-complete or `SomeClass.some_method?` in a notebook) than Google engineers who will go straight to the original source code to figure out what it does. Even if there is a maintenance cost associated with duplicating default values, it would be worth it to make interfaces more self-documenting.

No, IMO neither MAC header nor SFD make for good RX window definitions on PHY/L1 level. Your initial comment and definition (the one that I also used now) represents exactly my understanding of a low-level RX window and I believe this is how the method is actually being implemented right now - at least in Zephyr.

Why not MAC header? "receive at" is below MAC level (L1/PHY). CSL channel samples are part of a MAC protocol (one of many IEEE 802.15.4 MAC sub-protocols). A generic "receive at" feature should not know anything about MAC level. It is true that CSL phase refers to start-of-MAC but that is very CSL specific and happens nowhere else in the standard. This definition should be converted to low level "receive at" in the MAC.

Why not SFD? Defining an RX window relative to SFD only works for very specific PHY configurations. IMO that would make the actual start of the RX window completely non-deterministic, as even within one PHY (e.g. SUN FSK) there may be several SHR lengths. And if your receiver supports several PHYs at the same time then you're completely lost.

IMO SFD is for RX/TX timestamps (including timed TX) - not more not less.

Because, from a language theory it strikes me as plain wrong that we have something like `BaseService._run` as *private* **and** *abstract* at the same time. In fact, I don't know any *statically typed* (which Python isn't but there more we use mypy the more we try to be haha) that would allow this.

In something like Java or C#, if you want subclasses to be able to overwrite a method, or even force them to, then that method can not be private.

And it has real consequences, it means that if we document the `BaseService` like this:

```
.. autoclass:: p2p.service.BaseService
  :members:
``` 

Then, `_run` and `_cleanup`, probably the most important methods of this class, those that need to be overwritten, simply fall out of the documentation because they are incorrectly seen as private. Something that would be impossible in most if not all statically typed language that has the concepts of `private` members and inheritance.

>  Why do we need to search for the best result throughout history

We don't. The current implementation more or less does

``` python
score = {k: v[-1]["score"] for k, v in results.info.items()}
best_model_id = max(score, key=score.get)
```

The previous implementation picked the best model ID before searching through history.

> Is this information that _fit could just give us directly?

I refactored `_fit` to return information that's very useful in calculating that information, but not that information (the "best" model ID?) directly.

In this PR, `_fit` gives us model history separated by model ID for every model (not only the final models `_additional_calls` selects) in `results.info`. This is really useful: it allows getting the final scores for *every model* with

``` python
info, models, history = fit(...)
final_scores = {k: v[-1]["score"] for k, v in info.items()}
best_model_id = max(final_scores, key=final_scores.get)
```

The truncation behaviour, however, was (still is) untested and implicit. Fixing this properly entails at the very least documenting the truncation behaviour.

@sandcha suggests emitting a truncation warning at execution; I think that does not achieve the purpose of avoiding nasty surprises, because whoever is coding an input variable won't exert control on the actual inputs specified, and whoever actually specifies an overlong input is unlikely to see debug logs from the computation engine.

My preferred solution would be to rename max_length to something like truncate_to_length, to make the behaviour explicit; and a unit test for the truncation behaviour would be ideal.

The old code would mark any run other than the most recent as `isRetried`.  While the reasons you identified ('worker-shutdown', etc.) can be a cause of a new run, it's also possible to get a new run with the `rerunTask` API method, even if the previous run was resolved as successful.  So I think the modification you've made would miss that case.  Also, there's a limit to the number of retries a task will do (usually 5), so a task resolved as `worker-shutdown` might *not* be retried if it that run was the last allowed run.

That said, I don't know if this is worth worrying about.  If "isRetried" indicates that a task was *automatically* retried, then arguably it shouldn't be set for `rerunTask` anyway.  But I think the full solution would be one of the two alternatives you mentioned.

> I'm not sure I understand the reshape part of it. After masking bits you have two arrays of the same shape right (one for the cloud mask and one for the quality mask)?

The QA is 1km resolution, whereas the cloud mask, we are interested in, has a resolution of 250m. 

The information of the 250m resolution cloud mask is stored in the 8 bits of the fourth and fifth bytes of the `Cloud_Mask` dataset ([c.f. file description](https://modis-atmos.gsfc.nasa.gov/sites/default/files/ModAtmo/MYD35_L2.C6.CDL.fs)). For one "pixel" of the 1km resolution cloud mask, stand 16 (4x4) pixels for the 250m resolution dataset. Those pixels are organized as follows: 
- byte 4 bit 0 : Element(1,1)
- byte 4 bit 1 : Element(1,2)
- ...
- byte 5 bit 7 : Element(4,4)

Therefore, we need to reshape the original datasets to get a final array whose dimension are `4*SCAN_WIDTH` and `4*SCAN_LEN`. 

I have found the following working code in @BENR0's branch. The question is how to avoid hard coding it in the reader. 

```
# Cloud mask is stored in two bytes we need to recombine in one dataset
dataset_1 = dataset[4, :, :]
dataset_2 = dataset[5, :, :]

# We need to extract the value of each bit and return an array of relevant size

# Concatenate the two bytes
dataset_1 = np.uint16(dataset_1)
# Shift bits to the left
dataset_1 = np.left_shift(dataset_1, 8)  # dataset_1 << 8
dataset = da.bitwise_or(dataset_1, dataset_2).astype(np.uint16)

# Create 4x4 mask with numbers from 1 to 16 (count of bits) for extraction
bit_start = np.arange(16, dtype=np.uint16).reshape((4, 4))
# Tile the 4x4 array the size of the input array dimensions
bit_start = np.tile(bit_start, (dataset.shape[0], dataset.shape[1]))
# Replicate bits
bit_data = np.repeat(np.repeat(dataset, 4, axis=0), 4, axis=1)
# Decode the corresponding bits to get the 250m final dataset
dataset = bits_strip(bit_start, 1, bit_data)
```

These are not trivial changes to make correctly. I don't think we should alter this specific class without compelling reasons.

The entire design of the API here was honestly quite deliberate. For example, I specifically allowed copying because the initial code used `pid_t` everywhere and this ensured semantic equivalence and minimized the changes of bugs being introduced. Requiring unique ownership would require a significant refactor of the rest of the codebase to accommodate it. If someone feels compelled to do it and can do it without introducing bugs, I suppose they can go ahead, but it is a significant effort and in the end there seems to be very little to gain.

Note that the primary intention for this class is to provide an easy-to-use interface that works correctly across all operating systems. While there _are_ certain features missing that could be added and potentially come in handy, the limited interface here was primarily designed to satisfy our actual needs without adding unnecessary maintenance burden. Process management is possibly the biggest source of pain when porting code across operating systems, and every change would have to be shown to work correctly on all 3 platforms with uniform semantics.

Overall, this is one of the few classes classes where it's not a good idea to change it just for the sake of elegance. If there is a functionality we actually *need*, then perhaps that warrants adding it. But as long as it is working correctly and other solutions can be reasonably implemented on top, it should probably be left alone.

We can make the contrary: give 'frame_rate' the software value and add a new key (say, 'frame_rate_avg') for the average one.

gamma is not obsolete, in the sense that it was not removed by VisionResearch, but it has been replaced by f_gamma (f for float I suppose, see doc pp. 21 and 48). Both could be kept.

Sure. I added #7068 in attempt to make code like this easier to understand. Possibly more can still be done.

Essentially, the `run1()` call does one thing on the eventloop—if there's a callback scheduled on the eventloop it calls that. If there aren't any call backs scheduled but there are pending gRPC calls, it waits for a gRPC call to finish and then runs its callback. Basically, this is the function that causes "something" to happen on the event loop where eventually one of those things will cause one of the futures we're waiting on to finish, and then we'll return that future.

I think the most confusing thing about the event loop is that it's not really an event loop in the way we typically think about it. Generally, if we're writing event driven code, everything we write is an event handler and the event loop is always running and calling into our event handlers. In NDB, on the other hand, the event loop is really just a task queue that we poke (by calling `run1()`) whenever we want something out of it. So, the flow of control is inverted, really, if you think about it. Maybe it shouldn't be called an "event loop". 

The one and only time the event loop actually loops is when we call `run()`, which we only do when we're wrapping up our context, to make sure anything in the queue gets run before exiting the context. You can imagine someone might have fired off a `put_async` without waiting for a response, but even if the calling code doesn't need the result, we still have to make sure it gets run.

> Well, if the future needs Span then you'll need something different… a SpanProcessor if you want, and a different mechanism. Doing a one-size-fits-all design is not going to help us maintain a proper, robust and optimized system. 😞

Yeah, no I'm not a fan of trying to guess what the future may hold. I think really the problem right now is that processors can be chained together _and_ the processors may change the format of the data returned. Trace filters break the processor model because the type of data it consumes is dependent on a processor.

I think the right move would be that trace filters become part of the `SpansToTraceProcessor` (#2038). Processors then only act on spans and no state is passed between processors. The only problem with this is that the current writer wouldn't make sense - it would have to be owned by a processor/be a processor. This is roughly the model that opentelemetry has.

> Am I right if I understand you're trying to design this to also work for span streaming, where we could process on a per-span rather than a per-trace basis?

Yes, ideally I think we'd only want to do on a per-span basis.

> If span streaming is on, any filter/processor based on trace won't work anymore? If I have a filter that drops every trace made of more than 200 spans, how would I do that with span streaming?

Correct, trace filters won't work in a span streaming case. The trade-off would be that you don't have 200+ span traces kept in memory. I'm not sure if trace filters are something we want to support long term as right now they don't work with async/partial flushing due to traces being split up (this is fixed for async in #2038).

Does it? The code sample below that line explains it more clearly:

```python
# Validate request
uri = 'https://example.com/post_authorize?client_id=foo
headers, body, http_method = {}, '', 'GET'

# Fetch the credentials saved in the pre authorization phase
from your_datastore import fetch_credentials
credentials = fetch_credentials()

# Fetch authorized scopes from the request
from your_framework import request
scopes = request.POST.get('scopes')

from oauthlib.oauth2 import FatalClientError, OAuth2Error
from your_framework import http_response
http_response(body, status=status, headers=headers)
try:
    headers, body, status = server.create_authorization_response(
        uri, http_method, body, headers, scopes, credentials)
```

The `uri` passed to `create_authorization_response` in the example is clearly not the `redirect_uri`, its the URI of the current request. , `oauthlib` doesn't use the `uri` as the `redirect_uri`, it either extracts it from the uri or from the passed in `credentials`. We pass it in the `credentials`, which sets `request.redirect_uri` as an attribute on the `oauthlib.Request`

https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/b69fa53fd836dc559aa7fcd78ce075bcbe361629/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/endpoints/authorization.py#L89-L95

Checking the redirect uri uses this request.redirect_uri attribute, not the request URI.

https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/b69fa53fd836dc559aa7fcd78ce075bcbe361629/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/grant_types/base.py#L228-L241

Here are examples of how other libraries using oauthlib are handling this, from [flask-oauthlib](https://github.com/lepture/flask-oauthlib/blob/d8369c8ae06150d37da2eba951ae9646235f940b/flask_oauthlib/provider/oauth2.py#L493-L496):

```python
        uri, http_method, body, headers = extract_params()
        try:
            ret = server.create_authorization_response(
                uri, http_method, body, headers, scopes, credentials)
```

and from [bottle-oauthlib](https://github.com/Refinitiv/bottle-oauthlib/blob/d696c8388a8c499cb307f865f9264db47873cf6d/bottle_oauthlib/oauth2.py#L249-L255):

```python
                uri, http_method, body, headers = extract_params(bottle.request)
                scope = bottle.request.params.get('scope', '').split(' ')

                try:
                    resp_headers, resp_body, resp_status = self._oauthlib.create_authorization_response(
                        uri, http_method=http_method, body=body, headers=headers, scopes=scope
                    )
``` 

and from [oauthlib's test cases](https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/b69fa53fd836dc559aa7fcd78ce075bcbe361629/tests/oauth2/rfc6749/endpoints/test_client_authentication.py#L84-L90):

```python
        # implicit grant
        auth_uri = 'http://example.com/path?client_id=abc&response_type=token'
        self.assertRaises(ValueError, self.mobile.create_authorization_response,
                auth_uri, scopes=['random'])


        self.validator.validate_client_id.side_effect = self.set_client_id
        h, _, s = self.mobile.create_authorization_response(auth_uri, scopes=['random'])

Tried to fix that with:
```
if isinstance(load_pattern, (list, tuple)):
```
IMHO this `try/except` is as good as it gets. It receives the three types of inputs we'd like to put there, and it's rather efficient when compared to an `if isinstance(load_pattern, (str, list, tuple)`. That could also bring us issues in a more difficult type of input:
```
load_pattern = [(1, 'one'), (2, 'two')]
In []: isinstance(, (list, tuple))                     
Out[]: True
```

This is generally an anti-pattern.  `try/except` blocks int almost all situations should be as small as possible.  Large blocks like this often result in errors like accidentally catching exceptions that you don't intend to and then handling them even though they aren't the thing you intended to handle.

- have a build B we need to verify
- it's created from a decision task D1
- it's run in a docker image from docker image task DI1
- that docker image task was generated from a decision task D2
- that decision task was built from a docker image from docker image DI2
- ...

Then we have the potential for a long chain, and we have to trust something at the beginning somehow.

A very long explanation why this changed.

* Metadata on GitHub currently includes functions with `'codegen_method': 'no'`
* When I update the metadata soon (I am currently working on this) then those functions will no longer be added to the metadata on GitHub and will just go away.
* Before this change, we only expanded functions that were in the API (I.e. part of `Session`).
* After the change, we need to expand functions that are not part of the API, but are also _not_ `'codegen_method': 'no'`. I.e. `'library-only'`

I could have modified the code generator even more to avoid these changes, and I would have if the changes would have become permanent, but they aren't.

Once the metadata is updated without the unused functions, the documentation (and the note) will go back to the way they were.

It seemed foolish to torque the code generator around just to avoid temporary changed to the documentation.

```
@unittest.skipIf(
    os.name == 'nt',
    'Open file limit tests disabled for Windows')
class TestSetOpenFileLimit(unittest.TestCase):
```

> Actually, multi-threading is already a major issue with the disk-based cache, since I have to find a way to synchronize file accesses.

Yes.  A single file will require synchronization.  Avoiding lock/synchronizations can also boost the cache storage performance by many folds.  `list.txt` is essentially an index file.  We can even rename it appropriately in later iterations.  It contains metadata about stored item in a random order i.e. stored indexes are not ordered by any attribute.  We can also see `list.txt` as serialized index store as indexes exists in the order of requests.

Currently, we don't have an update flow i.e. once written, cache is immutable and never updated.  But, design wise we must prepare for an update cycle :) where cache is updated either based upon time based expiry, query parameters etc.  This decision making can be moulded into cache plugin lifecycle callback.

> I used a `multiprocessing.Lock()`, but I am not sure it is the best solution out there. Indeed, the reader can access the file simultaneously, while the writes must access the file sequentially. A "read-write lock" would be more appropriate. Or we could go for OS file locks, but that would be platform-dependent.

The task of on disk based storage leads us into database designing wonderland :) Good thing is our cache storage is pluggable and we can easily add new storage mechanisms _(leveldb, sqlite, cloud storages, ....)_

Few thoughts around approaching disk based storage using zero-dependency philosophy:

1) Storage:  We are currently caching responses as a single file per request.  So storage becomes pretty straightforward.  But for a long running server or with lots of data, I do expect us to run into situations where OS starts to run out of file descriptors _(possibly fixable by tweaking kernel parameters)_.  Let's not bother about it for now as fixing this is relatively easier if we get our indexes right.

2) Indexes:  Without any ordering on stored indexes, we indirectly increase one or both:
a) Startup time:  Cache plugin is busy ordering the indexes in-memory (if we decide to pre-warm the cache index).
b) Lookup time:  Cache plugin is busy iterating over unordered indexes (if we decide to consult index file for every read).

In short, ordering (fully or partially) indexes on disk can greatly benefit overall performance.  Thinking of primitive ways :) maintaining one index file per alphabet e.g. `a-list.txt`, `b-list.txt`, .... where each file store data about domains starting with respective alphabet (or any unicode character).  Such partitioning strategies can greatly reduce the lock contention across workers.

At some point we can also use an in-memory cache e.g. LRU cache to minimize disk hits.  Ideally, I would like to have a running copy of indexes in-memory but that is not always practical with lots of indexes (and not enough available RAM).

3) Synchronization: There is in-build `pubsub` mechanism in `proxy.py`.  Try it independently here https://github.com/abhinavsingh/proxy.py/blob/develop/examples/pubsub_eventing.py.

For some background, eventing mechanism was developed for `devtools` integration.  This allows `proxy.py` traffic to be inspected using `chrome devtools`.  `devtools` expects a `websocket` connection sending stream of events as described in `devtools` protocol specification.  In short, we do have a mechanism to produce a single stream of custom events from various worker processes.  Most importantly, eventing mechanism relieves us from synchronization pains.  

An idea could be to simply publish cache payload as events from within cache plugin.  Then, a single global subscriber thread or multiple subscriber threads (one per worker process) can consume events and write indexes to disk either synchronously (as events are received) or in batches.

Hopefully, we can mix some of the above ideas to build our own storage layer :)

The problem with the current code happens when a process is stopped and cleaned up, but the server is not wiped. You then end up in a state where there is a partial reset (counter value is lowered just by the amount of requests handled by the now stopped and cleaned up process) which can cause large issues with `rate` calculations.

How a partial reset is handled is that if a counter goes from 100 to 80 on cleanup (partial reset) it is assumed by Prometheus that the counter went from 100 to 0 to 80, causing a big spike during that period of time, when actually no requests may have come through.

Thank you for this comment it's worth thinking through and considering alternatives.

1. I find it awfully convenient to be able to add hand-authored files into the code-generated test tree, specifically test files defining unit (mock) tests over the generated code. Being in the same tree makes it easy to open up that tree in an editor and for it to feel like a normal project when editing those tests. So I would like to keep this capability.

2. The point you bring up is very important also - the rewrite procedures should completely remove files generated from a previous test, otherwise we cannot trust that we are actually checking the code-gen. Before the change, this was done by removing the folder whole-sale, which was trivially correct but conflicting with *1*. After the change, this is relying on `.sdkcodegenignore` patterns to teach the system which sub-trees to leave, assuming (but not knowing for sure) that no code-generated files would match the `.sdkcodegenignore` patterns. This also has the side-effect of leaving build files like node_modules alone when doing codegen rewrites, which is hopefully benign (can be removed by `git clean -fxd`).

How else could we accomplish 1+2?

a. Identify code-generated files by headers "// this file was generated", though this is problematic with json files

b. Make the code-generator emit a list of files "generated.txt" when generating, and clean only those

c. Slight variation on (1) where we copy hand-authored files from a "mixin" folder, such as:

```
rm -rf $codedir
mkdir $codedir
generate-code --to $codedir
cp -r $codedir-mixin/ $codedir/ # copy test files into the tree
cd $codedir && go test # etc
``` 

I personally think we might be ok as-is for now, but option (b) is attractive as it is just as ergonomic and has files in one place while being fully precise. Thoughts?

> Here is the callback we are using now

Now I see what the problem is. There is one case when callback_role.py is executed not directly by Patroni, but from the shell script https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/master/postgres-appliance/scripts/configure_spilo.py#L275.
When Patroni is [killing](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/1239/files#diff-452cfdd642bafda8a084b3557b9dcc9aL23) the currently running script, it just terminates the parent process, but all subprocesses continue to run. After that it executes the callback script one more time. BOOM! We have got two instances of callback_role.py running.

There is a good reason why we choose to terminate the old instance of the callback. Depending on how it (callback script) implements retry, it might be quite a long-running process. While it is running the Patroni state already changed and it doesn't make much sence to continue, because it just delays the correct callback.

The good solution I could think about is:
1. In the `call()` method collect all offsprings of the callback process
2. kill the callback process (it is already there)
3. kill offsprings
4. In the `run()` method wait for the callback process to finish (it is already there)
5. After that wait for offsprings to finish (if there are any)

ls python_package:

No, for a lock to be effective you have to create `1` lock and use it for all threads. If you create and use a different lock for each thread then such locks won't have any effect.

I see how this could be misinterpreted but if here `self.lock` is `None` this means that we didn't have to create any locks and therefore aren't using multiple threads. Hence we can just call the function directly.

Regarding speed, using reshape instead of view increases the latency to `4%` on GPU (K80) and `11%` on CPU.
Note that this reshaping is called only `1` time for each batch. Meanwhile repeat_interleave_with_expand is called a lot more than that (I noticed it is around `50` times for a batch I tried).
Code comparison:

```
import timeit
set_up_line = """
import torch as pt
pt.manual_seed(1)
heads = 8
batch = 20
target_max_len = source_max_len = 50
a = pt.rand(20, 8, 50, 50)
"""
executive_line = """
b = a.reshape(batch * heads, target_max_len, source_max_len)
"""
timeit.timeit(executive_line,setup=set_up_line, number=10000)
#cpu: 0.031618487962987274
#gpu: 0.013913217990193516

executive_line = """
b = a.view(batch * heads, target_max_len, source_max_len)
"""
timeit.timeit(executive_line,setup=set_up_line, number=10000)
#cpu: 0.028475572005845606
#gpu: 0.013381681987084448
```

> If an example is broken, and some bogus value is assigned to a property (e.g. a string to a boolean - before we started converting them, or a non-existing value because of the bug in HCL), why do we still generate those examples?

In general, we don't convert examples that don't typecheck: e.g. we would not accept an example that passes a list where a string is expected. That said, let's break down the two situations you mentioned, as the second is an exception to the rule.

In the first case, the values aren't actually bogus: [HCL2 performs automatic conversion of these values at runtime](https://github.com/hashicorp/hcl/blob/hcl2/spec.md#type-conversions-and-unification). The rewrite you added matches that behavior; we'll eventually need similar codegen for dynamic conversions of non-literal values.

In the second case, we tolerate missing variables because in some cases they are intended: parts of an example that are not relevant to the resource at hand are often elided. In these cases, we treat the missing variables as having the type `dynamic`, which allows them to pass typechecking.

In the end we would have 3 env variables:

- `DD_TRACE_DEBUG` - whether `logging.getLogger("ddtrace").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)` is set.
- `DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE` - adds a log handler pushing logs into this file
- `DD_TRACE_LOG_FILE_LEVEL` - sets the log level to push into the file, defaults to "DEBUG"

Things that I think make this work... the log file will not log debug logs until you set the level on the root logger.

That is, doing this:

```python
import logging


root = logging.getLogger("ddtrace")

handler = logging.StreamHandler()
handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
root.addHandler(handler)


assert root.isEnabledFor(logging.DEBUG) is False
root.debug("test")  # Does not get logged
```

Even though the handler is there and set to debug it does not log anything.

Unfortunately, `response_key` only accepts a string right now, as you can see. We could make it accept a callable, and then these helpers would be replaced with this usage:
```python
ADD_USER_FIELDS: FIELD_LIST_T = [
    ("Group ID", "group_id"),
    ("Added User ID", "identity_id"),
    ("Added User Username", "username"),
]

...

formatted_print(
    ...,
    response_key=lambda x: x["add"][0]
)
```

If you're interested in doing that here, to support this command, I'd be onboard with it. You basically need to change the use of `response_key` to check `callable(response_key)` and do little else.

I could also show examples in Python Markdown that use None, consider [`codehilite`](https://github.com/Python-Markdown/markdown/blob/a767b2daaad78ba32d45a4f1dabb7c5e218f030a/markdown/extensions/codehilite.py#L280).  Probably more important, we have an example in the very file you are editing [here](https://github.com/facelessuser/pymdown-extensions/blob/main/pymdownx/highlight.py#L72).

Providing a specific type like boolean will cause Python Markdown to type check the options. Why Pyhton Markdown does not type check integers is anyone's guess, but usually, when providing an optional parameter, `None` is [used](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.Optional), so this makes more sense to me and is my personal preference for this project.

```
        if not self._url_matches(self.url, request.url, self.match_querystring):

        if not self._body_matches(self.match, request.body):
            return False
```

GDAL doesn't have something like a module-level mapping to get the human-readable types, and the mapping of `gdal.GRA_*` to human-readable names is [local to the `gdal.WarpOptions` parsing function.](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/bfe1cfea364a0e185a792db67db7de2fc6aad902/swig/python/osgeo/gdal.py#L854).  I put together something in 7bdd1dbc8a4fc62015c0a737e4712b5022b75708 to get those human-readable names out of `gdal.WarpOptions` (avoiding `ast` inspection in case we can't read the source code like in pyinstaller binaries) and making them available at the module level.  This should make pygeoprocessing resilient to any changes to the set of resample algorithms GDAL offers, so long as they keep `gdal.WarpOptions` up to date!

Historically the 'tentative' class was needed:
* Peer messages are not processed at all ('SHOULD NOT') when delta-tKSC > 1. 
* Tentative messages are processed (called 'partially processed') when delta-tKSC > 1. 
* So the discovery type messages like Link Request / Parent Request / Announce would work even if the recipient was way behind with tKSC.  Useful to merge back long-separated partitions for example.

But now with new text we updated the 'peer' class to allow some processing when delta-tKSC > 1, as long as it doesn't change internal state. So that would allow all the discovery type messages to be processed and responses to be sent back.

This seems to remove one of the biggest reasons to have the 'tentative' class. Another minor reason might be: 
* current 'tentative' messages are all typically followed by further actions using MLE (unicast) messages; 
* effectively postponing any tKSC updates to later during the interaction.  
* While I do see the logic of this (don't let a discovery type message cause side-effects);
* it doesn't seem to have much practical benefits.

The sentence in the whatsnew is not exactly correct. In this PR, the `calcparams_*` functions decide the Python type of the return values checking these conditions in order:
- If `effective_irradiance` and `temp_cell` are scalars (as determined by `np.isscalar`), then return values are scalars.
- If either `effective_irradiance` or `temp_cell` is a `pd.Series`, then return values have type `pd.Series`.
- If either `effective_irradiance` or `temp_cell` is a `nd.ndarray`, then return values have type `np.ndarray`. This includes 0d-arrays.

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(ServiceException, self).__init__(*args)

        if len(args) >= 1:
            self.message = args[0]
        else:
            self.message = ""
         
        for k,v in kwargs.items():

Not destroying the container of `boost::dll::shared_library` is a great point. Anytime we need to have a static function local and it is not trivially destructible, it is recommended to store a pointer so it is never destructed. The argument is that otherwise if there is another thread reading the variable, the read can race with the static local variable's destruction.

What I have here matches our existing model for empty blocks, i.e. creating a null block metadata from the empty block. The only thing that we'd ideally want to preserve here is the schema, but that's not being preserved in other spots in which empty blocks are being created, and I think it's difficult to always preserve the schema in some situations (or at least would be a nontrivial change of operation implementations and maybe even the block data model).
```python
blocks: List[Block]  # Zero or more blocks
# Apply operation on zero or more blocks, which returns a new block.
# NOTE: If new block is empty (empty Arrow table, empty Python list), the schema
# of the input blocks are lost.
new_block = op(*blocks)
# Derive new block metadata from (possibly empty) new block.
new_metadata = derive_metadata_from_block(new_block)
```

> But also, are we sure we want this at all? Why not use the handler name the user configured directly?

Yes, I think so. The `BUILD` file target _must_ contain (most of) the entry point (at least the file/module part of `entry_point="path/to.py:handler"`), in order to compute dependencies/build the package, and it's annoying to have to carefully reproduce that elsewhere (`handler: 'parent.modules.path.to.handler'`), and synthesising a fixed module to incorporate the info pants already knows avoids that.

For instance, in our repo, we use AWS CDK, and have a generic `PythonLambda` construct where the basic "get the code running" config is just pointing it to the package (e.g. `foo.bar/target.zip`), because it can just set `handler: 'lambdex_handler.handler'` to get things running. I think this is really nice!

(There's obviously often per-lambda config required like any extra env vars or permissions, but CDK/the infra configuration is the source of truth there, it's not just directly duplicated from the `BUILD` files.)

> I think this is a good opportunity to get rid of this name, since we're churning anyway. It'll be harder to do later.

Sure, I'm happy to choose another name, e.g. some options:

- `lambda_function`: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/python-handler.html suggests AWS configures `lambda_function.lambda_handler` by default, when creating a Python function using the console
- `index`: using "inline code" in AWS's CDK uses `index` as the file name (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cdk/api/v2/docs/aws-cdk-lib.aws_lambda.Function.html#handler), but... the `handler` property always needs to be set
- `pants_lambda` or `__pants_lambda` or some other pants-y name

I think we can also make this customisable in future.

The test code itself is great! but I have a few suggestions:

* Let's remove unused imports
* Let's run `black` code formatter 

The following is an example that fixes all the above:

```python
import logging
import os
import unittest

from integration_tests.env_variable_names import (
    SLACK_SDK_TEST_BOT_TOKEN,
    SLACK_SDK_TEST_WEB_TEST_CHANNEL_ID,
)
from slack_sdk.web import WebClient


class TestBookmarks(unittest.TestCase):
    """Runs integration tests with real Slack API testing the bookmarks.* APIs"""

    def setUp(self):
        if not hasattr(self, "logger"):
            self.logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
            self.bot_token = os.environ[SLACK_SDK_TEST_BOT_TOKEN]
            self.sync_client: WebClient = WebClient(token=self.bot_token)
            self.channel_id = os.environ[SLACK_SDK_TEST_WEB_TEST_CHANNEL_ID]

    def tearDown(self):
        pass

    def test_adding_listing_editing_removing_bookmark(self):
        client = self.sync_client
        # create a new bookmark
        bookmark = client.bookmarks_add(
            channel_id=self.channel_id,
            title="slack!",
            type="link",
            link="https://slack.com",
        )
        self.assertIsNotNone(bookmark)
        bookmark_id = bookmark["bookmark"]["id"]
        # make sure we find the bookmark we just added
        all_bookmarks = client.bookmarks_list(channel_id=self.channel_id)
        self.assertIsNotNone(all_bookmarks)
        self.assertIsNotNone(
            next(
                (b for b in all_bookmarks["bookmarks"] if b["id"] == bookmark_id), None
            )
        )
        # edit the bookmark
        bookmark = client.bookmarks_edit(
            bookmark_id=bookmark_id,
            channel_id=self.channel_id,
            title="slack api!",
            type="link",
            link="https://api.slack.com",
        )
        self.assertIsNotNone(bookmark)
        # make sure we find the edited bookmark we just added
        all_bookmarks = client.bookmarks_list(channel_id=self.channel_id)
        self.assertIsNotNone(all_bookmarks)
        edited_bookmark = next(
            (b for b in all_bookmarks["bookmarks"] if b["id"] == bookmark_id), None
        )
        self.assertIsNotNone(edited_bookmark)
        self.assertEqual(edited_bookmark["title"], "slack api!")
        # remove the bookmark
        removed_bookmark = client.bookmarks_remove(
            bookmark_id=bookmark_id, channel_id=self.channel_id
        )
        self.assertIsNotNone(removed_bookmark)
        # make sure we cannot find the bookmark we just removed
        all_bookmarks = client.bookmarks_list(channel_id=self.channel_id)
        self.assertIsNotNone(all_bookmarks)
        self.assertIsNone(
            next((b for b in all_bookmarks if b["id"] == bookmark_id), None)
        )
```

Agree that this is weird, but I think that this weirdness is just inherited from the weirdness of @logger. In a world where there were only handlers, the two responsibilities would be very distinct: DagsterLogManager is a log that produces messages with Dagster metadata and has a set of handlers on it. DagsterLogConversionHandler is a special handler that sneakily converts normal python logs into Dagster-formatted logs, and then calls all of DagsterLogManager's handlers with this new record.

Things only get tricky because we need to handle the case of taking a record and `emit`ing it, and also taking a record and `log`ing it. However, I can refactor this such that the handler doesn't need to have a back-reference to the DagsterLogManager, which might make things a little cleaner.

Here we make 2 deferent jobs:

- `BuildRpmPackageCentOS7`
- `BuildRpmPackageCentOSStream8`
